results.possibleIdentity: William Stevenson "Bill" Hawkey was a New York-based art director, photographer, and multi-image artist active in the late 1960s and throughout the 1970s. He was a prominent figure in the multi-image production field, a medium that utilized synchronized slide projectors and computer technology to create immersive visual experiences, a technology closely aligned with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Hawkey was part of the "Incredible Slidemakers" New York crew, a group of pioneers in high-tech audio-visual shows. He is the author of the book "Living With Plants" (1974) and worked extensively in the advertising industry. He is married to the renowned advertising executive Penelope "Penny" Hawkey and is the father of actress Molly Hawkey and musician Renn Hawkey.

results.possibleIdentity: Jay Bouton was a designer and architect active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. In 1971, he was identified as a senior student and subsequently a graduate student at the Pratt Institute in Brooklyn, New York. During his time at Pratt, he was involved in interior design and architecture projects, including research for a master's thesis focused on prison reform, which was cited in the AIA Journal in November 1971. Originally from Denver, Colorado, Bouton's early career in New York coincided with the peak activity of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), a non-profit organization that fostered collaboration between artists and engineers. By 1989, he had established an international design practice based in Hong Kong called Design Phase Ltd. (also referred to as designphase limited), where he specialized in hotel and office design throughout the Pacific Rim, including projects in Burma and Thailand. He was also a partner in the firm Brown Raymond Boulton Szabo.
